coredata.dc:description: Agricultural coatings play a crucial role in addressing the global food crisis by significantly improving the plant growth environment, accelerating plant development, and enhancing the biological quality of plants. Nevertheless, there exist constraints in the form of limited sunlight utilization, decreased light transmission caused by surface icing leading to diminished plant photosynthesis, and high cost. In this study, a cost-effective and readily scalable superhydrophobic photothermal light-conversion coating is suggested as a means to improve the light conditions necessary for optimal plant growth. By employing in situ growth of carbon dots (CDs) on the surface and interlayers of montmorillonite (MMT) and hydrolytic polymerization of fluorinated alkyl silane (FAS) on the surface of MMT, light-converting superhydrophobic materials (CDs/MMT) with a micro-nano structure were synthesized. These materials demonstrated broad-spectrum fluorescence emission suitable for utilization by plant photosynthesis. They were dispersed into an epoxy resin (ER) matrix to obtain an agricultural superhydrophobic coating (ER-CDs/MMT). The “light absorption-capture-conversion” effect confers exceptional passive anti-icing and active de-icing properties upon the coating. Furthermore, outdoor planting experiments have demonstrated its capacity to substantially enhance the light environment and enhance the biological quality of soybean and morning glory by leveraging light scattering and conversion characteristics. This underscores its considerable potential for application in the next generation of facility agriculture.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Agricultural coatings play a crucial role in addressing the global food crisis by significantly improving the plant growth environment, accelerating plant development, and enhancing the biological quality of plants. Nevertheless, there exist constraints in the form of limited sunlight utilization, decreased light transmission caused by surface icing leading to diminished plant photosynthesis, and high cost. In this study, a cost-effective and readily scalable superhydrophobic photothermal light-conversion coating is suggested as a means to improve the light conditions necessary for optimal plant growth. By employing in situ growth of carbon dots (CDs) on the surface and interlayers of montmorillonite (MMT) and hydrolytic polymerization of fluorinated alkyl silane (FAS) on the surface of MMT, light-converting superhydrophobic materials (CDs/MMT) with a micro-nano structure were synthesized. These materials demonstrated broad-spectrum fluorescence emission suitable for utilization by plant photosynthesis. They were dispersed into an epoxy resin (ER) matrix to obtain an agricultural superhydrophobic coating (ER-CDs/MMT). The “light absorption-capture-conversion” effect confers exceptional passive anti-icing and active de-icing properties upon the coating. Furthermore, outdoor planting experiments have demonstrated its capacity to substantially enhance the light environment and enhance the biological quality of soybean and morning glory by leveraging light scattering and conversion characteristics. This underscores its considerable potential for application in the next generation of facility agriculture.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Greenhouse cultivation is one of the most important parts in modern facility agriculture. For greenhouse planting, the light conversion agricultural film is of great importance [1–3]. The solar radiation permeating the interior of a greenhouse can be categorized into Ultraviolet (UV), Photosynthetic Active Radiation (PAR), and Near-Infrared (NIR). UV radiation is divided into three bands: UV-A (320–400 nm), UV-B (280–320 nm) and UV-C (100–280 nm), which can inhibit leaf enlargement, stem elongation and biomass production. UV-B radiation also can damage organic combination bonds and thus greenhouse cladding [4–6]. The smaller wavelengths are more dangerous. Photosynthetically active radiation (PAR) ranges from 400 to 700 nm and shows the highest utilization of blue light (400–500 nm) and red light (590 nm–700 nm) [7]. NIR (780 nm–3000 nm) is main cause of increasing greenhouse air temperature that less absorbed by the plants while absorbed by the construction elements of the greenhouse [8]. Based on this, UV-blocking and NIR-blocking covers have been developed and seem to be promising but resulting in waste of these light [9]. Converting UV (down-conversion) and NIR (up-conversion) radiation to PAR is a more potential way to utilize an even broader range of the solar spectrum. Rare earth (RE) doped materials [10–12], fluorescent dyes [13] and quantum dots (QDs) [14–16] are frequently investigated for the light transformation by means of photon down- and up conversion [17]. The inorganic rare earth nanomaterials are only excited by ultraviolet (UV) light. In general, a combination of two or more rare earth fluorescent materials are required to achieve both down-conversion and up-conversion. However, the expensive cost limits the addition quantity of rare earth in polymer matrix. Fluorescent dyes are easy to be bleached and degraded under solar radiation, shortening the service life of light conversion agricultural films, and increasing their application cost. In recent years, carbon dots (CDs) as a new class of fluorescent nanomaterials have attracted much attention due to high photostability, ease of synthesis, low toxicity, tunable luminescence, and solution-processability [18]. Most of them are fluorescence on the visible spectrum extending into the near infrared, up-conversion luminescence/anti-Stokes luminescence (UCPL) also makes it possible to convert NIR light into visible light. Interestingly, the different chemical and physical functions of CDs can be tailored by embedding them in a solid matrix for macrostructural modulation and by spatially arranging the matrix containing multiple CDs, such as assembly and aggregation induced by surface interactions such as hydrogen bonding, metal coordination, electrostatic or π–π conjugation [19,20]. The current research on the use of CDs as smart light conversion materials with simultaneous UV (down-conversion) and IR (up-conversion) excitations for plant growth applications is still lacking.

Furthermore, in cold winter, snow or ice are frequently attached to the greenhouse film. surface will cause overweight of the film and decrease the transmittance of solar light that lead to film damage and low photosynthetic efficiency. In recent years, researchers have proposed some methods to prevent or delay the freezing of droplets on the surface of the substrate. One is known as active deicing methods, consist of ice removal from surfaces using external energy, such as mechanical and thermal loads [21]. Another is passive methods which involve chemically or physically modifying the surface to achieve rapid separation of water droplets from the substrate and delayed icing, which in turn reduces the adhesion of ice to the substrate [22,23]. Superhydrophobic surfaces as a typical passive anti-icing surface is one of the most available and promising ways. Some micro/nanostructures are considered as a way to achieve superhydrophobicity to further improve the anti-icing performance [24]. This has stemmed largely from the fact that hierarchical micro/nanostructured topography provides an air layer between the solid surface and the liquid droplets, and the layer, accordingly, reduces the actual contact area and thus prevents thermal conduction [25]. However, at low temperatures, droplets can readily penetrate through an air cushion above the microstructures and fully infiltrate into the interiors of the microcrater structure. This triggers the transition of droplets from a Cassie-Baxter state to a Wenzel state, thereby leading to the ice self-locking phenomenon [26]. Obviously, a single passive anti-icing strategy can only delay the water condensation time. Once the droplets on the superhydrophobic surface cannot be removed timely and it freezes, the anti-icing performance is expected to fail. Therefore, some effective strategies of combining with active de-icing and passive anti-icing have been developed, such as photothermal or electrothermal and magnetic superhydrophobic materials. A variety of photothermal superhydrophobic materials have achieved superior de-icing ability [27–29]. However, the majority of the materials that have been reported to generate a photothermal effect rely on the high light absorptivity of dark colored materials such as carbon [30,31]. These materials are highly opaque, resulting in a complete lack of transmittance of incident sunlight required for photosynthesis. This presents a significant obstacle in promoting plant growth. Only a limited number of studies have explored the use of transparent or semi-transparent superhydrophobic surfaces for effective photothermal conversion [32]. However, transparent photothermal coatings without a light-conversion function do not provide plants with access to utilize broader range of solar wavelengths, and cannot convert UV and NIR radiation that resulting in an inhibition of plant growth, moreover, overly transparent coatings easily cause sun burn of leaves. This has been rarely studied. Therefore, there is a significant need to explore the development of a novel material that possesses moderate transmittance, photothermal properties, and light conversion integration in order to enhance plant growth. However, limited research has been conducted in this area thus far.

Herein, we present a straightforward and easy scale-up method to prepare light-converting photothermal superhydrophobic materials (CDs/MMT) (Fig. 1 a). This involves the in-situ growth of CDs on the surface and interlayer of MMT through a one-step hydrothermal process. These CDs were capable of converting UV light and NIR light to PAR by the integration of up-conversion and down-conversion luminescence. 1H,1H,2H,2H-Perfluorodecyltriethoxysilane (FAS) and the nanosheets of MMT established a hierarchical micro-nano structure with low surface energy, a durable superhydrophobic coatings (ER-CDs/MMT) based on mixture of epoxy resin (ER) and CDs/MMT was fabricated by a simple spraying approach. It enabled enhanced capability of photothermal conversion of 98.6 °C under 635 nm laser (1.0 W/cm2) and favorable superhydrophobic feature of 165°, showing excellent anti-icing and deicing performances. Furthermore, as a demo, it could be also used as a light-converting coatings to efficiently foster bean sprouts growth. The light scatting effect of the coating increased the distribution area of inside sunlight and reduces the radiation intensity to prevent leaf burns, demonstrating a promising potential in agricultural system.

o-Phenylenediamine (o-PDA) were obtained from Aladdin Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Montmorillonite (MMT) was bought from Wuhan Kanos Technology Co., Ltd. Citric acid, Concentrated nitric acid, acetone, Epoxy resin (E51) and curing agent (T403) were supplied by Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. γ-(2,3-Epoxypropoxy) propyltrimethoxysilane (KH560) was supplied by Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. (3,3,4,4,5,5,6,6,7,7,8,8,9,9,10,10,10-Heptadecafluorodecyl) trimethoxysilane was acquired from Bide Pharmatech Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China).

Montmorillonite (MMT, 0.5 g), 1H,1H,2H,2H-Perfluorodecyltriethoxysilane (FAS, 0.4 g), citric acid (CA, 0.275 g), o-phenylenediamine (o-PDA, 0.3 g) were dissolved in C2H5OH (50 mL), and then transferred to a Teflon autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 8 h. The CDs/MMT suspension were dried at 70 °C for 12 h to obtain the powdered superhydrophobic CDs/MMT.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of light-converting photothermal superhydrophobic coating ER-CDs/MMT
ce:section.ce:para: The epoxy resin (ER, 0.9 g), CDs/MMT (0.1 g), curing agent (0.3 g) and KH560 (0.1 g) were premixed in 20 mL of acetone solvent, mechanically stirred until well mixed, and then the mixture was sprayed onto the surface of the pretreated glass plate with a spray gun to form a superhydrophobic ER-CDs/MMT coating. The same procedure was used for ER-CDs/MMT coating of simulated greenhouse. Other specific characterization and experimental method are shown in the Supporting Information.

Construction of the simulated greenhouse (dimensions: 35 × 30 × 35 cm3): the base is made of white andy board; the top, left, right and back are made of transparent organic board; the bottom of the front is made of a Styrofoam sheet to block the loss of nutrient soil, and the rest of the front is the same as the atmosphere to ensure oxygenation of the plants. A commercial planting soil of about 5 cm thickness was placed at the bottom of the greenhouse, and soybean and petunia seeds were placed on the surface of the soil. After spraying with water, the soil is covered with a layer of 0.5 cm thick.

Carbon dots (CDs) were synthesized by solvothermal method using citric acid (CA) and o-phenylenediamine (o-PDA) as carbon and nitrogen sources. The morphology of the CDs and the composite CDs/MMT were characterized using transmission electron microscopy (TEM). In Fig. 1b, CDs were well dispersed, homogeneous spherical particles, with average diameters of about 3.07 nm. High-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image showed well-resolved lattice fringes of 0.21 nm lattice spacing calculated by fast Fourier transform (FFT) calculations (Fig. 1c, Fig. S1), consistent with the (100) plane of graphene [33]. As shown in Fig. S2, the TEM image of CDs/MMT composites shows the layered structure of MMT. From the SEM image, MMT is amorphous with a smooth surface (Fig. S3); while the composite CDs/MMT present a rough surface with the presence of in situ-grown CDs (red circles) (Fig. 1d). The EDS mapping images of CDs/MMT show that the N element is derived from the amino group on the surface of CDs, the Si element is derived from the silica-oxygen tetrahedra in MMT, and the presence of the F element indicates that the CDs/MMT were successfully fluorinated during the process of reducing the surface energy (Fig. S4), demonstrating that CDs were successfully grown in situ to build a dense nanoscale rough structure on the surface of MMT. X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern indicates that 001 peaks of MMT and CDs/MMT at 7.0° and 5.5° respectively (Fig. 1e). The layer spacing was calculated to be d001 (MMT) = 1.26 nm, while the addition of CDs expands it to d001 (CDs/MMT) = 1.60 nm according to the Bragg equation [34]:
D sin θ =

λ ( n = 1 , 2 , 3 ; λ = 0.154

This indicates that some CDs of small particle size are inserted into the layers of the MMT. All the above results can indicate the successful synthesis of micro- and nanostructured superhydrophobic composites CDs/MMT.

The interaction between CDs, FAS and MMT was investigated using FT-IR (Fig. S5). The spectrum of CDs shows a CO group stretching vibration at 1667 cm−1 is mainly derived from the carboxyl groups of CA. N-H antisymmetric stretching vibration peak at 3415 cm−1 originates from o-PDA. The skeleton of the benzene ring vibration peaks at 1452 cm−1 and the characteristic peaks at 738 cm−1 of meta-substituted benzenes indicates that CDs retains the original structure of the o-PDA. For MMT, the stretching vibration of –OH attached on Si and Al is at 3630 cm−1 and the peak at 3445 cm−1 refers to the absorbed H2O deformation on the layer surface. Peak at 1040 cm−1 can be attributed to lattice Si-O and Si-O-Si vibration. The characteristic peaks of CDs and MMT simultaneously appeared in the spectrum of CDs/MMT. The strong absorption band at 3630 cm−1 became rather weak after hydrolysis-polymerization reaction between FAS and MMT. The absorption peaks at 917 cm−1 (Al-Al-OH deformation) nearly disappeared because of the complexation of Al and CDs. Furthermore, hydrogen bonds could be formed between the carboxyl/amine/hydroxyl groups of the CDs and the hydroxyl groups of MMT during composites fabrication. Hydrogen bonds also formed between FAS and CDs through C-F group and carboxyl/amine/hydroxyl groups.

The functional groups of CDs/MMT were further detected in depth through X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis. The XPS spectra of the sample CDs/MMT clearly reflected the XPS peaks of C 1s, N 1s, F 1s, O 1s, and Si 2p. The high-resolution C1s XPS spectra (Fig. S6a) shows six binding energy peaks at 293.4 eV, 291.0 eV, 287.4 eV, 285.6 eV, 284.7 eV, and 283.9 eV, which were attributed to –CF3, –CF2, CO, C–N, C–C/CC, and C-Si [35]. The N 1s spectrogram shows two peaks at 400.3 eV and 399.1 eV attributed to graphitic N, and pyrrolic N (Fig. S6b) [36]. The XPS spectrum of the high-resolution F 1s has only one peak with a binding energy of 687.9 eV attributed to −CF2 (Fig. S6c) [37]. The O 1s spectrogram shows two peaks at O-Si and O-Al binding energies of 532.7 eV and 532.0 eV (Fig. S6d) [38,39]. In addition, the Si 2p region includes a peak at 103.4 eV and a peak at 102.8 eV, which are from Si-C and Si-O-Si, respectively (Fig. S6e). As displaced in Fig. S6f, The XPS full-spectrum image shows that 832 eV to 860 eV belongs to the fluorine KLL Auger electron peaks. Together, these analyses demonstrated the successful in-situ growth and intercalation of CDs to MMT, and the modification of FAS.

A series of spectroscopic analyses were carried out to investigate the optical properties. The absorbance spectra of CDs/MMT (Fig. 2 a) displays a broad absorption band at 300–600 nm. The ultraviolet (UV) part of the absorbance spectrum at 320–340 nm has generally associated the features in such a ectral range to π–π* transitions of CC from sp2 benzene skeleton in CDs. The absorption peak at 370–400 nm is caused by the n-π* transitions of the CO groups [40]. In addition, the absorption properties in the visible region (400–700 nm) are usually attributed to the defective states due to the abundant oxygen- and nitrogen-related functional groups attached to the edges of CDs. Next, the CDs/MMT were subjected to a large-scale fluorescence scan. As shown in Fig. 2b, c, significantly, the photoluminescence (PL) spectrum showed multiple fixed down-conversion fluorescence emission peaks at 483 nm, 485 nm, 517 nm, 596 nm, and 630 nm. It is excitation-independent without any blueshift or redshift impunity, meaning that the CDs has a high environmental stability and a rather simple excited state relaxation channel. This multiple color emissive CDs have largely been the results of different extent of graphitization. CDs grown in MMT interlayers have lower graphitization degree than which grown on the surface of MMT. The precursors o-PDA molecules with sp2 hybridized structure connect CA with sp3 hybridized structure through carboxyl groups to form a sp2/sp3 hybridized domain. The sp2 hybridization is associated with yellow and red emissions, while the sp3 hybridization is linked to blue-green emissions. The appropriate aromatic structure present in o-PDA leads to a reduction in the energy gaps of CDs by creating extensive sp2 domains via dehydration and carbonization reactions. Additionally, the incorporation of nitrogen-doping from amino groups within the o-PDA backbone, as well as the introduction of surface –COOH groups, can further decrease the band gap, resulting in a red shift and the attainment of red emission.

Encouragingly, up-conversion fluorescence emission peaks at the same wavelength with down-conversion were detected at the 630–800 nm excitation range (Fig. 2c). It is also lack of dependence of the emission spectrum on the excitation wavelength. The up-conversion photoluminescence properties observed in this study can be explained by the multiphoton active process, wherein the concurrent absorption of two or more photons results in the emission of light at a shorter wavelength compared to the excitation wavelength (anti-Stokes emission). It opens up a possibility of broadening the available range of sunlight for plant photosynthesis. The excitation spectra were examined at 480 nm and 595 nm, with results (Fig. S7) indicating excitation potential within the 300–800 nm range. Decay kinetics were analyzed through time-resolved fluorescence spectroscopy, yielding a characteristic fluorescence lifetime of 8.90 ns (Fig. 2d). This determination was made by fitting experimental data to a triple-exponential decay equation, detailed in the Supporting Information. Additionally, the photoluminescence quantum yield was found to be 13.43 %.

The prepared CDs/MMT were mixed with ER and spray onto the substrate to obtain a superhydrophobic coating ER-CDs/MMT. CDs/MMT nanoparticles are the major contributors to the hierarchical structure, while ER are responsible for tightly anchoring the nanoparticles to the substrate to ensure the strong integrity of the coating. The micro-nano structure and the low surface energy modification by FAS collectively confers super-hydrophobicity to the coating's surface. The wettability of the ER coating, ER-MMT coating and ER-CDs coating was also examined for comparison (Fig. S8). Static water contact angle (WCA) and sliding angle (SA) images of the coatings are shown in Fig. S9. The WCA of bare ER coatings is 117.2° and SA is more than 20°. Both ER-CDs coating (WCA = 113.5°) and ER-MMT coating (WCA = 72.9°) are more hydrophilic than ER coating and SA is also more than 20°. Combining the results of EDS, while FAS is added during the preparation of ER-CDs coatings, the essential micro-nano structures from MMT are absent, thus leading to a low WCA. As for ER-MMT coating without hydrophobic modification, though the micro-nano structure can be formed on the surface, the increased hydrophilicity due to hydrophilic groups –OH on MMT is more significant. According to Fig. 3 a and 3e, the WCA of ER-CDs/MMT was 165.1° and the SA was 1.6°. These changes indicate the remarkable superhydrophobic properties of the coating in the presence of CDs/MMT. The cross-sectional SEM analysis illustrates the rough structure morphology generated by the deposition of CDs/MMT on the substrate surface (Fig. 3c). Additionally, the self-assembly of the MMT sheets within the ER leads to the development of a multi-cavity micro-nano structure, as depicted in the accompanying Fig. 3b, these cavities facilitate the creation of an interfacial air layer between the solid and liquid phases, thereby mitigating liquid infiltration into the solid material. While for the ER coating and ER-CDs coatings, the fracture surfaces exhibit flat and smooth homogeneity (Fig. S10a, b).

The utilization of a 3D Confocal Laser Scanning Microscope (3D CLSM) visually elucidates the alterations in roughness resulting from the incorporation of CDs/MMT. The data of the roughness parameters such as Sq, Sp, Sv, Sz, Sa and Sdr were listed in Table S1. Sa stands for roughness. As displaced in Fig. S10c, it can be seen that the ER coating is almost all flat and smooth green color with lowest Sa 0.183 μm; after introducing CDs, there were a small number of micro-bumps formed on the surface of the coatings (Fig. S10d), and Sa increased to 1.186 μm. When CDs/MMT were added, dense peak structure appeared with Sa reached to 25.529 μm, which can reduce the contact area between the water droplet and the coating surface, and be helpful for trapping relatively more amount of air cushions for better water repellency (Fig. 3d). Furthermore, EDS mapping reveals a homogeneous distribution of fluorine elements, indicating the effective modification by FAS and the proper dispersion of additive phases within the composite coating (Fig. S11, S12).

To further conform the super-hydrophobic and adhesion characteristics of the four substrates, experiments were systematically conducted on the dynamic dragging and bouncing behaviors of microdroplets. After the super-hydrophobic surface was subjected to contact, compression with microdroplets at the needle, the droplets remained suspended, as depicted in Fig. 3f, indicating that the coating has an ultra-low adhesion to the water droplets. High-speed camera recorded the speeding bouncing behaviors. A successful bounce required the droplet to first drop and impact the substrate and then bounce off the substrate (Fig. 3g). The water droplet was able to perform three successful bounces within 180 ms on the ER-CDs/MMT coated surface, while the droplet was unable to bounce on any of the other three coated surfaces (Fig. S13). Benefiting from the rebound force obtained by squeezing the air captured in the micro-nano hierarchical structure and low surface energy molecules, the coating demonstrates exceptional super-hydrophobic and low adhesion properties.

In practical applications, the surface will inevitably become soiled by ubiquitous dust and contaminants, which can degrade properties such as light conversion. The self-cleaning property of the CDs/MMT surface was evaluated using quartz sand as a contaminant, as illustrated in Fig. 4 a. Due to the CDs/MMT coating's low roll angle of only 1.6°, water droplets added to the surface can readily roll in a spherical shape and gather contaminants under gravity. When the water dripped on the contaminated surface, the water droplets rapidly rolled and took away the contaminants, recovering a clean surface. In contrast, ER coatings have a high adhesion to water droplets, the droplets on ER are difficult to slip off and agglomerate with the contaminants, making it difficult to remove the contaminants (Fig. 4b).

Durability is especially important in outdoor application scenarios. The mechanical durability of the surface was studied by performing abrasive paper wear tests as shown. The coated CDs/MMT sample was placed face down on 600 mesh standard sandpaper at a load of 200 g and moved a distance of 20 cm for one abrasion cycle (Fig. S14).

In the reciprocating abrasion test, it is obvious that after 100 cycles the WCA values of the coating are slightly reduced due to partial removal of micro/nano roughness (Fig. 4c). Even after 200 cycles, the coating exhibited good superhydrophobicity (WCA = 157.2°, SA = 6.5°). Compared to previous reports, the coating retained better hydrophobicity after mechanical reciprocal abrasion (Table S2). In addition, the Taber abrasion test was used to evaluate the abrasion resistance according to the ASTM standard test. It was found that the coatings exhibited good superhydrophobicity even after 100 cycles (WCA = 159.6°, SA = 5.6°, Fig. S15). This robust superhydrophobicity can be attributed to the graded buildup of CDs/MMT on the surface, as shown in the cross-sectional SEM (Fig. 3c). As the top filler wears away, the next level of filler is exposed, ensuring the continued maintenance of the superhydrophobicity of coating.

As an agricultural coating, prolonged exposure to UV light is unavoidable. Thus, the surface was irradiated with 365 nm UV light for 24 h to evaluate the UV resistance, the WCA and SA were monitored at 4 h intervals (Fig. 4d). The results show that the hydrophobicity remained virtually constant during the full test, revealing the good ultraviolet resistance, which can be attributed to the UV-absorbing effect of CDs. Furthermore, to verify the usability of the coating in cold winters and hot summers, the coatings was subjected to −80 °C, −20 °C, 35 °C, 80 °C, 140 °C, and 200 °C for 1 h, then returned to room temperature and tested the WCA and SA. As shown in Fig. 4e, the coating retained their superhydrophobicity when exposed to extreme temperatures. Thus, the ER-CDs/MMT coating have excellent mechanical/chemical durability. In order to make the coating better integrated with modern agricultural infrastructures. We sprayed the coating onto agricultural PE film with the same formulation and still maintained its good performance. As shown in Fig. S16, we tested the PE@ER-CDs/MMT coating's WCA and SA, demonstrating that the substrate has a negligible effect on the coating's wetting properties. In addition, Fig. S17 demonstrates the bending performance of the coating on PE film. The coating still maintains its super-hydrophobicity after several bending and folding cycles. This also illustrates the excellent flexibility for the coating. The compact three-dimensional network structure created by the introduced siloxane coupling agent via hydrolytic condensation reactions additionally contributes to the structural stability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Superhydrophobic passive anti-icing and photothermal active deicing performance
ce:section.ce:para: The prominent photothermal property of the superhydrophobic surface enhances its de-icing capability. The photothermal performance of the superhydrophobic coating was investigated by a red-light emitter (635 nm, 1 W cm−2) and recorded using an infrared thermographic camera (Fig. 5 a, Fig. S18). The time dependence of the surface temperatures of ER, ER-CDs, ER-MMT and ER-CDs/MMT coatings was provided. Fig. 5b illustrates the correlation between irradiation time and temperature for ER, ER-CDs, ER-MMT and ER-CDs/MMT. The surface temperature of the ER coating exhibited minimal change and stabilized at approximately 27 °C after a 5-minute period of irradiation. The surface temperatures of the ER-MMT, ER-CDs and ER-CDs/MMT experienced a rapid increase within 30 s of irradiation. Subsequently, the temperatures of the ER-MMT and ER-CDs coatings remained relatively constant at 48 °C and 55 °C, respectively. In contrast, the ER-CDs/MMT coating demonstrated a swift rise to over 98 °C within 30 s of irradiation, reaching a maximum of 98.6 °C before stabilizing after approximately 60 s.

The photothermal effect observed in ER-CDs coatings can be attributed to the absorption of light at a wavelength of 635 nm by the CDs, and a portion of the energy is emitted as thermal radiation. ER-MMT coating lacks light absorption capacity; however, the cave-like micro-nano structure formed by MMT on the surface functions as a light trap. Incident light is reflected and scattered multiple times within these structures, enhancing light capture ability and generating heat in the process. For ER-CDs/MMT, by integrating CDs and MMT, a significant portion of light is absorbed by CDs and captured in the micro-nano structures. This captured light is swiftly converted into a substantial amount of heat within the light trap formed by a large number of interconnected micron and nano pores, giving ER-CDs/MMT superior photothermal performance. To further explore the photothermal stability of the coatings, continuous irradiation cooling experiments were conducted over five cycles of irradiation (5 min) and natural cooling (5 min). As shown in Fig. 5c, the ER-CDs/MMT coatings did not show any significant degradation trend in photothermal properties, indicating superior photothermal stability. According to the equation provided in the Supporting Information, the photothermal conversion efficiency (η) is calculated to be as high as 61.9 %, which present a superior photothermal conversion performance (Table S3).

In order to better evaluate the heat collection performance of the coating, comparative tests were conducted using a xenon lamp (1 kW/m2) to simulate sunlight and an infrared thermographic camera to record surface temperatures (Fig. S19a, b). Similar to the results of red laser irradiation, the photothermal conversion capability of the ER-CDs/MMT coating was significantly better than the other three groups. After 60 s of xenon irradiation, the ER-CDs/MMT coating reached temperatures of more than 80 °C and up to 82.7 °C. The ER-CDs, ER-MMT, and ER coatings only reached 50.7 °C, 45.1 °C, and 40.7 °C, respectively, after 5 min of irradiation at the same light intensity (Fig. S19c). As displaced in Fig. S19d, the continuous irradiation and cooling experiments further demonstrate the excellent photothermal stability of the ER-CDs/MMT coating. According to the formula provided in the “Supporting Information”, the photothermal conversion efficiency (η) reaches 60.98 %, which is similar to the η when irradiated by red laser light. The above indicates that the superhydrophobic coating has stabilized solar photothermal conversion performance.

Superhydrophobic surfaces have important application prospects in passive anti-icing due to their superior high water contact angle and low rolling angle. The passive anti-icing performance of coatings is evaluated by testing the icing delay time, which is defined as the time required for water droplets to completely freeze at a certain temperature. 100 μL water droplets were placed on the surfaces of ER and ER-CDs/MMT, respectively, and subsequently placed the samples in a refrigerator (ambient temperature: −20 °C). Record the freezing time of water droplets on different coating surfaces. As displaced in Fig. 5d, the water droplets initially appeared transparent and was semicircular in shape. The ER coating shows a small amount of ice crystals at 300 s, while ER-CDs/MMT does not appear until 800 s. The water droplets on ER coatings were completely frozen at 900 s, whereas for ER-CDs/MMT, the freezing time of water droplets was obviously prolonged to 1950s. As a result, the complete freezing time of water droplets on the superhydrophobic coating ER-CDs/MMT was delayed by approximately 2.2 times compared to the ER coating. This performance gap can be explained by the reduced thermal transmission efficiency between the droplet and the glass substrate. The presence of air cushions confined within micro/nanostructures on the surface of ER-CDs/MMT serves to diminish the solid–liquid interface area, consequently impeding the rate of heat transfer between water and the glass substrate. In general, a smaller contact area results in reduced heat transfer rates, leading to prolonged freezing times. From a thermodynamic perspective, the free energy barrier (ΔG) for ice nucleation is determined by [41,42]:
Δ G =
16 π
γ
LX
3
3 Δ
G V 2
2 + cos θ
1 - cos θ
2
4
in which γLX represents the liquid-nucleus interface energy, ΔGV denotes the free enthalpy per unit volume outside of the interface energy, and θ stands for the WCA. Thus, it can be inferred that a greater water contact angle corresponds to an increased free energy barrier for ice nucleation (ΔG), rendering the icing process more challenging.

Although superhydrophobic surfaces have the ability to retard icing, freezing still occurs when they are exposed to low temperatures for a long period of time, thus combining the photothermal effect with the superhydrophobic property can achieve self-deicing after icing. The CDs and micro- and nano-scale structure of ER-CDs/MMT surfaces triggers a “light absorption-capture effect” that gives the coatings excellent photothermal conversion capabilities. The photothermal deicing capability of the ER and ER-CDs/MMT coatings were evaluated by IR laser generator (635 nm, 1.0 W cm−2). As illustrated in Fig. 5f, approximately half of the ice sphere melted at 70 s, with only a minimal presence of ice crystals remaining at the apex by 200 s, ultimately culminating in complete liquefaction into a transparent droplet by 300 s. However, the ice on the ER surface did not completely melt until 900 s. In contrast, the superhydrophobic ER-CDs/MMT surface had a photothermal de-icing time that was three times faster than the blank ER surface. A xenon lamp (1 kW/m2) was used to simulate sunlight to evaluate the photothermal anti-icing performance of the coatings, we placed 100 μL water droplets on the coating surface and subjected it in a cryogenic environment for freezing. As presented in Fig. S20a, about 50 % of the ice droplets on the surface of the ER-CDs/MMT coating melted by 120 s and completely melted into transparent water droplets by 320 s under the xenon lamp (1 kW/m2) irradiation. In contrast, the ice droplets of the ER group with low photothermal conversion efficiency completely melted at 950 s (Fig. S20b), and the melting rate was three times slower than to the former. These results indicate that the photothermal de-icing performance of the ER-CDs/MMT coating is significant. In fact, the ice crystals grow rapidly and gradually occupy the gaps of the micro- and nanostructures. With the absence of photothermal properties, the ice on the ER only partially melts on the surface, whereas ice in the micro/nano structures is difficult to melt, resulting in a cascading effect that hinders the de-icing process. In addition, during the melting process of ice on the ER-CDs/MMT coating, the air layer in the contact region between the ice and the superhydrophobic surface is gradually restored due to the Marangoni force, resulting in the melted ice being easy to roll off, which facilitates the surface deicing process [43].

The thermodynamic perspective reveals the heat transfer model between the droplet and the surface of ER and ER-CDs/MMT samples as depicted in Fig. 5e. Existing studies have shown that in the absence of light, the droplets acquire heat from the air by radiation and conduction. At the same time, the droplets dissipate heat to the cold surface by the same approach. In Fig. 5e, the heat transfer equation for the ER and ER-CDs/MMT surfaces can be expressed as [44]:
Q =
Q g
-
Q l

In the context of this study, where Q is the net gain of the droplet heat per unit time, with Qg and Ql denoting the heat gain and heat loss, respectively. When Q < 0, the droplet is in a cryogenic state, signifying a loss of energy and eventual complete freezing of the droplet.

When exposed to light, the surface absorbs additional heat, leading to the formulation of the heat transfer equation as follows:
Q
∗
=
Q g
-
Q l
+
Q s
Where Q* represents the net heat increment of the droplet per unit time in the presence of light and Qs denotes the heat increment of the absorbed light per unit time. In the case of the coated surface, there is an additional heat increment from the photothermal substrate, which is characterized by the heat transfer equation:
Q
∗
=
Q g
-
Q l
+
Q s
+
Q
ST

QST represents the supplementary heat gain on the surface of a coating due to the photothermal effect. Therefore, in the presence of light, ER-CDs/MMT coating with the photothermal effect can provide more QST to the ice, thereby accelerating the melting of the ice.

In conclusion, the “light absorption-conversion” effect of CDs combined with the layered micro- and nanostructures on the surface of MMT nanosheets creates a “photothermal trapping” effect. For solar wavelengths (0.35–2.5 μm), pores of about micrometer size effectively trap and scatter solar light regardless of the wavelength, while nanopores of about hundreds of nanometers in size selectively scatter ultraviolet and visible wavelengths. The anti-icing coating contain a large number of interconnected micrometer and nanopores, which contribute to the reflectivity. As a result, anti-icing micro- and nanostructured coating exhibit higher diffuse reflectance than ER coating. In addition, a large number of CDs with strong light-absorbing properties are distributed in the micro- and nanostructures, thus helping to absorb more light in the “light-traps”. Thus, the micro- and nanostructure of the ER-CDs/MMT coating surface and the strong absorption of CDs give the coating excellent photothermal conversion properties and contribute to its photothermal de-icing performance.

Based on the superior spatial light regulation ability, the CDs/MMT can maintain a good convert of light in the working modes. For scale-up, ER-CDs/MMT superhydrophobic coatings were prepared separately on 35 × 30 × 35 cm3 transparent organic plates. As a proof of concept, outdoor soybean and morning glory planting experiments were designed to work on sunny summer days. Three greenhouses made of transparent Plexiglas were used as plant growth containers Fig. 6 a–c. The outer layers of the three greenhouses were blank coated without any material (Blank), sprayed with carbon dots coating (ER-CDs) and sprayed with superhydrophobic light-converting materials coating (ER-CDs/MMT). Each group was sown with the same number of soybean and morning glory seeds, and then the three sets of experimental greenhouses were placed outdoors in full sunlight with good air circulation. The three experimental groups were guaranteed the same conditions for all variables except for the different coating types.

Analyzes of alterations in indoor lighting subsequent to the application of the coatings were conducted. The transmittance of the three greenhouses was quantified through ultraviolet–visible-near infrared (UV–vis-NIR) spectroscopy (Fig. 6d). ER-CDs/MMT exhibits a moderate transmittance for plants growth. Excessive transmittance (90 % for Blank group) may lead to an overabundance of direct sunlight, resulting in potential plant damage due to sunburn. Furthermore, ER-CDs/MMT demonstrated reduced infrared transmittance compared to ER-CDs. If in a closed system, elevated infrared transmittance can directly result in heightened greenhouse temperatures, potentially inducing heat stress in plants. We tested the haze values of the coatings using a WGW photoelectric haze meter (Fig. S21). The haze of the ER-CDs/MMT coating was 35.03, which was higher than 12.23 in the blank group and 21.70 in the ER-CDs group. This phenomenon can be ascribed to the light scattering effect of the ER-CDs/MMT coating, which is a result of the presence of surface micro-nano structures. It deflects some of the direct light away from the angle of incidence, thus increasing the haze. However, this allows for a larger internal light distribution area while avoiding plant burns caused by excessive localized direct light. In order to mitigate the interference of plant shading on light sensor readings, we conducted a study monitoring light intensity in three sets of simulated greenhouses for six consecutive days (2023.09.05–2023.09.11) after removing the plants. The findings presented in Fig. 6e indicate that the light intensity observed in the ER-CDs/MMT group was notably higher than that of the Blank and ER-CDs groups during the midday period. This phenomenon can be ascribed to the light scattering effect of the ER-CDs/MMT coating, which is a result of the presence of surface micro/nano structures. When direct sunlight interacts with the surface, it undergoes multiple reflections and refractions within the micro-nano structures, ultimately leading to the scattering of light in all directions within the room. Consequently, this enlarges the spatial distribution of sunlight within the room, allowing the light sensor to detect light from various angles. Furthermore, the micro-nano structure of the surface has been shown to decrease dust adhesion, thereby maintaining a cleaner surface. In order to observe the light scattering effect of the coating more intuitively, a “dark box experiment” was conducted (Fig. S22a). A homemade box was lined with light-absorbing black cloth, and a light source was positioned on the aperture at the top of the box, which was covered with the sample. The results were captured by a camera at the side aperture of the enclosure. As shown in Fig. S22b, c, when light passes through the holes covered by the Blank and ER-CDs coating, there is almost no light scattering phenomenon and the light area distribution is small. Under identical light conditions, the light passing through ER-CDs/MMT results in a notable increase in the distribution area of light, revealing large-angle bright areas characterized by intense light scattering (Fig. 6f). In comparison to high light transmittance, light conversion and light scattering play a more significant role in promoting plant growth. In the Blank group characterized by excessive transmittance, soybean leaves exhibited prominent yellow spots and wilting in a period of approximately 20 days, which was due to the impaired accumulation of chlorophyll and carotenoids in plant leaves caused by prolonged exposure to excessive direct sunlight under too high transmittance, ultimately resulting in sunburn (Fig. 6a). In contrast, sunlight was subjected to the light conversion and light scattering properties of the ER-CDs/MMT coating, weakening the intensity of direct sunlight. In addition, the coating can convert harmful UV light and useless visible light into blue and red light that are favorable for plant growth, which can compensate for the lost light transmittance and allow plants to take advantage of a larger area of useful sunlight for photosynthesis. As a result, the transparency of the coating is appropriate and plant experiments have verified that plant grow better at this level of transparency. (Fig. 6c).

To quantify the growth of soybean and morning glory outdoors, we measured their naturally elongated stem lengths daily for one month (2023.07.11–2023.08.11). The growth curves of soybean (Fig. 7 a) showed that there was no significant difference in stem length among the three groups in the first 4 days, this is due to the fact that during this stage of growth, a significant portion of the soybean plant remains submerged in the soil, resulting in limited reception to light, thus ER-CDs/MMT had little growth-promoting effect on soybean during germination. The same conclusion can be drawn from the growth curves of morning glory in the first 3 days (Fig. 7b). Interestingly, the stem length of ER-CDs/MMT after 12 days in soybean was significantly longer than that of the other two groups. The morning glory in ER-CDs/MMT group entered the exponential growth period from 21 days onwards, which showed much higher stems than other groups. The above results indicate that the promotion effect of the ER-CDs/MMT coating on light-sensitive plants mainly occurs at the growth stage after germination. Because at this stage, the plant has developed leaves capable of absorbing photosynthetically active radiation converted by ER-CDs/MMT which converts harmful UV light and useless visible light into light useful for plant photosynthesis. For example, blue-red light that can promote chlorophyll accumulation, so the light-convert coating can increase the utilization rate of plant photosynthesis and thus promote the growth of green plants. After 30 days, soybean (Fig. 7g–i) and morning glory (Fig. 7j–l) plants were pulled out for quantitative analysis of chlorophyll content (Fig. 7c), stem length (Fig. 7d), stem diameter (Fig. 7e) and root length (Fig. 7f). The results showed that the chlorophyll content, stem length, stem diameter and root length of soybean and morning glory in the ER-CDs/MMT group were significantly better than those in the Blank and ER-CDs groups. For soybean in ER-CDs/MMT group, plant chlorophyll content increased by 20.0 % and 13.8 %, stem length by 21.17 % and 17.07 %, stem diameter by 26.0 % and 13.0 % as well as root length by 24.2 % and 23.08 % compared to those within the Blank and ER-CDs groups, respectively. For morning glory in ER-CDs/MMT group, the chlorophyll content increased by 9.51 % and 5.65 %, stem length by 100.74 % and 95.4 %, stem diameter by 23.32 % and 22.68 %, and root length by 20.56 % and 17.59 %, respectively.

In summary, with regard to plant species, two representative plants were selected for outdoor experiments in this study due to short growth cycles. Furthermore, soybean represented the crop species and morning glory represented the flowering plant species. The experimental results showed that ER-CDs/MMT significantly promoted the biological quality of two randomly selected representative plants. Therefore, it can be demonstrated that the coating has potential application for other plant species in terms of biological quality improvement. In addition, the environment has less impact on the performance of the coating, the summer season in Nanjing, China (2023.07.11–2023.08.11) was characterized by high temperatures and rain, with occasional gusty winds and thunderstorms. After a month-long outdoor experiment, the growth of soybean and morning glory in the ER-CDs/MMT group was significantly better than the other two groups. This demonstrates the excellent weathering performance of the ER-CDs/MMT coating. It can be analyzed that ER-CDs/MMT coating displays an appropriate and wide application prospect in improving the light conditions required for plant growth to enhance the biological qualities. In addition, we use montmorillonite as a raw material, which is a budget, easily available, green and pollution-free natural clay mineral. The whole preparation process is relatively simplistic and environmentally friendly. Furthermore, the CDs/MMT suspension with cost of 3.75UDS can generate 6–9 m2 superhydrophobic coatings. Thus, the coatings have excellent cost-effectiveness and scalability. All of these suggested that there is great potential for large-scale production of the ER-CDs/MMT coating toward practical applications.

In summary, we have presented a light conversion superhydrophobic coating in a scalable way based on the in-situ growth of CDs to MMT to construct micro-nano structures, enabling favorable light conversion and superhydrophobic properties. CDs/MMT exhibits a wide range of fluorescence emission without excitation wavelength dependence, and integrates the luminescence characteristics of up-conversion and down- conversion, which is beneficial to broaden the range of sunlight used by plants. The ER-CDs/MMT coating exhibits passive anti-icing and active photothermal de-icing capabilities as a result of the micro-nano hierarchical structure and the light absorption properties of CDs. Given these benefits, ER-CDs/MMT coatings have the potential to serve as an agricultural photoconversion coating for enhancing light conditions and stimulating plant growth. Results from outdoor cultivation trials involving soybean and morning glory plants demonstrate that the coating effectively enhances plant growth and development, leading to notable increases in plant stem length, stem diameter, root length, and chlorophyll content. Consequently, ER-CDs/MMT coatings exhibit promising prospects for improving crop quality, application in advanced facility agriculture practices and promoting the development of new agricultural facilities.
